planted_defects.defect: Consistency/contract: strict 'created_at < cursor' plus rows sharing created_at means boundary-timestamp rows are SKIPPED on the next page; orders silently disappear.
Omission: ORDER BY created_at has no unique tie-breaker (id), so ordering among equal timestamps is non-deterministic and pages are unstable.
Deep-reasoning: first-page default 'cursor ?? new Date()' uses the app clock; timezone mismatch or future-committed rows make 'created_at < now' silently exclude valid recent rows.

claudeCands.summary: Duplicate-cursor rows are silently dropped (strict `<` comparison on a non-unique key breaks the 'every order appears on exactly one page' contract).
claudeCands.evidence: The cursor is `created_at` of the last row on the page, and the next query uses `WHERE created_at < $1`. If two or more orders share the same `created_at` timestamp and straddle a page boundary, any rows with the same timestamp as the last-returned row are excluded from the next page. Example: page 1 ends with a row at T, but three other orders also have created_at = T that were not yet returned (LIMIT cut them off). The next page queries `created_at < T`, so those three orders are never returned on ANY page. This directly violates the stated contract 'every order appears on exactly one page.' created_at is not declared unique and timestamps commonly collide (bulk inserts, same-second creation). A correct keyset cursor must use a tiebreaker, e.g. `WHERE (created_at, id) < ($1, $2) ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC`.

claudeCands.summary: Non-deterministic ordering: `ORDER BY created_at DESC` alone has no tiebreaker, so rows with equal created_at can be returned in arbitrary, varying order — making pages unstable and the cursor boundary ambiguous.
claudeCands.evidence: SQL does not guarantee a stable order among rows with identical sort-key values. With `ORDER BY created_at DESC` and no secondary sort column, two queries can return ties in different relative orders. This means `page[page.length - 1]` (the row used to derive nextCursor) is not deterministic when the boundary falls within a group of equal timestamps, and the overall sequence of rows across pages can shift between requests. This violates the contract that 'pages are stable.' The fix is the same composite ordering/cursor as above (add `id` as a deterministic tiebreaker).

claudeCands.summary: Initial-call cursor default `new Date()` can skip rows created between the JS clock and DB insert time, and is subject to clock skew between app server and DB.
claudeCands.evidence: On the first call (`cursor == null`), the code substitutes `new Date()` (the application server's wall clock) into `WHERE created_at < $1`. Any order whose stored `created_at` is >= that JS timestamp is excluded from page 1 and, because subsequent cursors only move backward in time, will never appear on any page. This happens when created_at is set by the database (e.g. `DEFAULT now()`) and the DB clock is ahead of the app clock, or simply for rows inserted in the brief window after `new Date()` is evaluated. The first page should use an open/unbounded query (omit the WHERE clause when no cursor) rather than fabricating a client-side timestamp boundary.

claudeCands.summary: Cursor serialization/round-trip hazard: nextCursor is a Date object (or DB timestamp value), but if the client returns it as a string the `created_at < $1` comparison and resumption semantics can change, and sub-millisecond/timezone precision can be lost.
claudeCands.evidence: `nextCursor` is set to `page[...].created_at`, a raw timestamp value. Cursors are normally serialized to JSON (string) and sent back by the client. When the string comes back as `cursor`, it is bound to a `timestamptz` parameter; if the driver/timestamp lacks the sub-millisecond precision or timezone offset of the stored value, the `<` boundary can shift, causing either a skipped row or a repeated row at the boundary. JS `Date` also only has millisecond precision, so a Postgres `timestamp(6)` value with microseconds is truncated when it passes through `new Date()`/JSON, again moving the `<` boundary off the true row value. This is a latent correctness defect tied to the choice of a non-unique, precision-sensitive column as the sole cursor.

claudeCands.summary: `hasMore` / `nextCursor` inconsistency on the final page: when the last page exactly fills `limit`, hasMore is false but nextCursor is still a non-null timestamp, inviting a wasted (and potentially row-skipping) extra request.
claudeCands.evidence: `hasMore = rows.length > limit`. If the total remaining rows equal exactly `limit` (so the query returns exactly `limit` rows, not `limit+1`), `hasMore` is correctly false, but `nextCursor = page[page.length-1].created_at` is still non-null. A client that paginates on `nextCursor != null` rather than on `hasMore` will issue one more query with that cursor. Combined with the strict-`<` duplicate-timestamp issue above, that follow-up query is not just wasteful — if the boundary row's timestamp is shared, it can return rows that should have been considered already, or appear to return nothing while masking the duplicate-drop bug. nextCursor should be null when hasMore is false.

claudeCands.summary: Mutating shared default: every null-cursor call allocates `new Date()` at call time inside the argument expression — correct per-call, but note the boundary is evaluated once and is exclusive, so the very first row exactly at `now()` (created concurrently) is excluded by `<` rather than `<=`.
claudeCands.evidence: Using strict `<` against `new Date()` means an order created at exactly the same instant as the computed cursor on the first page is excluded. While millisecond collision with the client clock is unlikely, combined with the clock-skew point it widens the window of first-page rows that can be permanently skipped. This is the boundary-exclusivity facet of the first-page bug and reinforces that the first page should be unbounded rather than `< new Date()`.

claudeCands.summary: No handling of new rows inserted during pagination is fine for newest-first, but DELETED or backdated rows are not — a row whose created_at is later edited, or insert with an out-of-order/backdated created_at after page 1, can be missed or duplicated, breaking 'exactly one page'.
claudeCands.evidence: The contract claims every order appears on exactly one page and pages are stable, but the design only holds if created_at is immutable and monotonically assigned in insert order. If an order is inserted with a backdated created_at (e.g. data import, manual correction) after a client has already paged past that timestamp, it will never be seen. Conversely, if created_at is updated upward between two page fetches, the same order can appear on two different pages. The function provides no snapshot/consistency guarantee, so the stated contract is not actually upheld under realistic mutation patterns.

claudeCands.summary: Non-unique cursor (created_at) silently skips/omits orders that share the same timestamp at a page boundary, violating the 'every order appears on exactly one page' contract and hiding records.
claudeCands.evidence: The query uses `WHERE created_at < $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC` and `nextCursor` is set to the last row's `created_at`. If multiple orders share an identical `created_at` (common with bulk inserts, batch jobs, or coarse timestamp resolution) and that value straddles a page boundary, the strict `<` comparison drops ALL remaining rows with that timestamp. Example: a page ends on one of three rows timestamped `12:00:00.000`; the next query asks `created_at < '12:00:00.000'`, permanently excluding the other two rows so they appear on NO page. The fix is keyset pagination with a unique tiebreaker, e.g. `(created_at, id) < ($1, $2)`. This is a data-integrity defect: orders silently vanish from the listing.

claudeCands.summary: Unvalidated/unbounded `limit` enables resource-exhaustion DoS, and non-numeric `limit` corrupts the hasMore/page logic.
claudeCands.evidence: `async function listOrders(cursor, limit = 20)` accepts `limit` with no validation, then uses `limit + 1` in `LIMIT $2` and `rows.length > limit` / `rows.slice(0, limit)`. A caller forwarding a request value (e.g. `req.query.limit`) can pass `limit = 1e9`, forcing the DB to materialize a billion rows and exhausting memory/CPU. There is no integer coercion, no positive-value check, and no `Math.min(limit, MAX)` clamp. A string `limit` makes `limit + 1` do string concatenation (`'201'`) or yields `NaN`, and `NaN > limit` plus `slice(0, NaN)` further corrupt `hasMore` and the returned page. `limit` must be coerced to a bounded positive integer.

claudeCands.summary: Missing access-control / tenant scoping: `SELECT * FROM orders` has no owner/user/tenant filter and the function receives no principal identity, so any caller can page through ALL orders (broken access control / IDOR).
claudeCands.evidence: The query is `SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at < $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT $2` — the only constraint is a time bound. There is no `user_id = $owner` / `tenant_id = $tenant` scoping, and the signature `listOrders(cursor, limit)` carries no principal/owner identity, so it cannot enforce row-level authorization. For a multi-user `orders` table this returns other customers' orders to any caller. This is OWASP A01 broken access control.

claudeCands.summary: The `cursor` is untrusted, unsigned client input fed straight into the timestamp comparison and round-tripped to the client, allowing arbitrary seeking into the global ordering and unhandled type errors on malformed values.
claudeCands.evidence: `cursor ?? new Date()` passes the raw `cursor` directly to `WHERE created_at < $1`, and `nextCursor` is returned to the client (`return { orders: page, nextCursor, hasMore }`) to be fed back. Because the cursor is an unsigned client-controlled value, a caller can supply any `created_at` to jump to an arbitrary point in the ordering (compounding the missing tenant scope by enabling enumeration of arbitrary time ranges). Parameterized `$1` prevents classic SQL injection, but there is no type/format validation, so a malformed cursor (`{}`, a bad string) either throws information-leaking errors or, under loose timestamp parsing, returns an attacker-chosen slice. The cursor should be validated as a well-formed timestamp and ideally opaque/signed.

claudeCands.summary: `cursor ?? new Date()` bounds the first page by the app server's wall-clock now; combined with strict `<`, newest orders with `created_at >= now` (clock skew, future-dated/scheduled orders) are excluded from every page, again breaking the one-page contract.
claudeCands.evidence: On the initial call `cursor` is nullish so `$1 = new Date()` (app-server time). Any order with `created_at >= now` — possible from clock skew between app server and DB, future/scheduled orders, or rows committed in the same millisecond after `new Date()` is evaluated — fails `created_at < $1`, so it never appears on the first page; since pagination only walks backward in time, it never appears on any later page either, violating 'every order appears on exactly one page'. Newest-first pagination should use a sentinel that cannot exclude the legitimately-newest rows (omit the predicate on the first page, or use a strict keyset tiebreaker).

claudeCands.summary: Non-unique created_at timestamps cause rows to be silently skipped or duplicated, breaking the 'exactly one page' contract. The cursor uses strict inequality (created_at < $1) on a column that is not guaranteed unique. If multiple orders share the same created_at value and that value straddles a page boundary, the next query excludes ALL rows with that timestamp — including ones not yet returned.
claudeCands.evidence: Concrete failure: suppose limit=2 and rows have created_at = [10:00, 09:00, 09:00, 09:00, 08:00] (three orders at 09:00). Page 1 returns the first two (10:00, 09:00) and sets nextCursor = 09:00 (the last seen row's timestamp). Page 2 runs WHERE created_at < '09:00', which excludes ALL three 09:00 rows — the two un-returned 09:00 orders are permanently lost from results. They appear on ZERO pages, violating 'every order appears on exactly one page'. Conversely, if the cursor used <= to avoid loss, the already-seen 09:00 row would reappear, causing duplicates. With a non-unique ORDER BY key there is no correct choice; a tiebreaker column (e.g. ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC with a composite cursor) is required.

claudeCands.summary: First-page cursor default 'new Date()' silently drops orders with future or clock-skew timestamps, and is non-deterministic. When cursor is null/undefined, the default is the current wall-clock time, so any order whose created_at is >= now (future-dated, or inserted by a node with a slightly fast clock, or created in the millisecond gap before this query runs) is excluded from the very first page and from all subsequent pages.
claudeCands.evidence: Line `[cursor ?? new Date(), limit + 1]` with `WHERE created_at < $1`. An order with created_at slightly in the future relative to the querying server's clock satisfies `created_at < new Date()` as false and never appears on page 1 (the only page that could include the newest rows), violating the 'exactly one page' contract. It is also non-deterministic: two callers requesting 'the first page' microseconds apart get a different boundary, so the same order can fall on different sides depending on timing — pages are not stable across the null-cursor entry point.

claudeCands.summary: Pages are not stable when new orders are inserted between page fetches — the contract's 'pages are stable' claim is false for the newest-first scheme. Because new rows are inserted at the TOP (newest created_at) while pagination walks downward via created_at < cursor, inserts during a pagination session do not shift already-fetched pages, but the boundary semantics mean a row inserted with a timestamp equal to or near a prior cursor can be skipped or the page contents change between two reads of 'the same' page.
claudeCands.evidence: If a client re-fetches page 1 (cursor=null) after new orders arrive, page 1's contents differ from the first fetch (new top rows pushed in), so 'pages are stable' does not hold for re-reads. More subtly, a row inserted with created_at exactly equal to an in-flight nextCursor value will be excluded by the strict `<` on the subsequent page just like the tie-collision case. The contract asserts stability but the design only achieves append-stability for rows strictly older than every issued cursor, not the stability the comment claims.

claudeCands.summary: hasMore can be true while nextCursor points to a position that yields an empty or partial next page under concurrent deletes, and the hasMore flag is computed before slicing in a way that can disagree with reality. hasMore is derived purely from fetching limit+1 rows at query time; if the (limit+1)th row (or the tie-group it belongs to) is deleted before the next fetch, the caller is told hasMore=true but the follow-up query returns nothing.
claudeCands.evidence: `const hasMore = rows.length > limit;` reflects state at this query's instant only. A client trusting hasMore=true will issue another request with nextCursor and may get an empty page (orders=[], hasMore=false). While not a hard contract break, combined with the strict-`<` tie handling it means the extra (limit+1)th row used only to compute hasMore is discarded (`page = rows.slice(0, limit)`), and its created_at is never used — so if that discarded row shares a timestamp with page[last], the duplicate/skip boundary problem above is invisible to hasMore.

claudeCands.summary: Empty-result pagination produces nextCursor=null but with no way to distinguish 'end of data' from 'temporary empty window', and a cursor of an exact-match boundary can loop. When a page returns zero rows (e.g. cursor older than all rows, or all rows in the window deleted), page.length is 0 so nextCursor=null and hasMore=false — correct for end-of-data, but a caller that passes a hand-constructed or stale cursor equal to the oldest row's created_at gets the oldest row excluded by strict `<`.
claudeCands.evidence: `const nextCursor = page.length ? page[page.length - 1].created_at : null;` returns null on empty pages. If a caller persists a cursor equal to the single oldest order's created_at and re-paginates, `WHERE created_at < $1` excludes that oldest order entirely — it appears on no page. The oldest row in the entire table can only be returned if a cursor strictly greater than its timestamp is used; any cursor saved AS that row's timestamp permanently hides it.

claudeCands.summary: Type/serialization mismatch on the cursor: created_at returned from the DB and re-supplied as $1 may be a string vs Date, and `cursor ?? new Date()` does not validate or coerce, risking incorrect comparisons or query errors across the page boundary.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor is set to `page[page.length - 1].created_at`, whose JS type depends on the driver (often a Date, but JSON round-trips through an API as an ISO string). On the next call that value is passed straight into `[cursor ?? ...]`. If the client serialized it (string) and the driver/column expects a timestamp, comparison semantics or precision (sub-millisecond/microsecond truncation in JS Date) can shift the boundary, causing rows at the truncated-microsecond boundary to be re-included or skipped. The function performs no normalization of the cursor type.

claudeCands.summary: Contract violation: orders with duplicate created_at values are silently dropped (lost between pages), breaking 'every order appears on exactly one page'. The cursor advances using strict `created_at < $1`, but created_at is not guaranteed unique. If multiple rows share the same created_at and that timestamp falls on a page boundary, every other row with that same timestamp is skipped entirely on the next query.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor = page[last].created_at. Next call runs `WHERE created_at < $1` (strict <). Example: 21 rows all at time T, limit=20. Page 1 returns 20 of them, nextCursor=T. Page 2 queries `created_at < T` → returns 0 rows (the 21st row, also at T, is excluded by strict <). That order appears on ZERO pages. The contract demands exactly one. A correct keyset cursor needs a tiebreaker (e.g. ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC and a composite `(created_at, id) < ($1, $2)` comparison).

claudeCands.summary: First-page cursor default `new Date()` excludes any order created at or after the moment of the call, and is non-deterministic. Because the default is `cursor ?? new Date()` combined with strict `<`, an order whose created_at equals or exceeds the server's current clock at call time is invisible on page 1 — violating 'every order appears'. It also makes the first page time-dependent rather than 'newest-first' of all rows.
claudeCands.evidence: `[cursor ?? new Date(), ...]` then `WHERE created_at < $1`. An order with created_at == now (or a future-dated/clock-skewed row) satisfies neither `< now`, so it never appears. The newest order can be silently omitted. A newest-first first page should have no upper bound at all (omit the WHERE clause when no cursor), not bound it by wall-clock now.

claudeCands.summary: `hasMore` and `nextCursor` are inconsistent: on the final page hasMore is false but nextCursor is still a non-null timestamp, so a caller that paginates on `nextCursor != null` (rather than on hasMore) issues one extra wasted query, and a caller mixing the two signals gets contradictory state.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor = `page.length ? page[last].created_at : null` — it is non-null whenever the page is non-empty, regardless of hasMore. On the last page hasMore=false but nextCursor!=null. The two returned termination signals disagree about whether pagination is done. Conventionally nextCursor should be null exactly when hasMore is false (`const nextCursor = hasMore ? page[last].created_at : null`).

claudeCands.summary: 'Pages are stable' is violated by interleaving inserts/updates. New rows inserted with a created_at between existing page boundaries shift content across page boundaries between requests; the keyset approach reduces but does not eliminate this, and the comment claims unqualified stability.
claudeCands.evidence: The function offers no snapshot/consistency mechanism (no fixed as-of timestamp, no MVCC snapshot id). With `WHERE created_at < cursor` and concurrent inserts at timestamps below the cursor, the set of rows on not-yet-fetched pages changes between requests. 'pages are stable' holds only for the already-emitted prefix, not for the whole listing as the comment implies.

claudeCands.summary: `SELECT *` is inconsistent with the cursor abstraction: the row shape returned to callers is undefined/coupled to schema, and `created_at` must be a directly comparable/serializable value for the cursor to round-trip — a JS Date vs DB timestamp string mismatch can break `created_at < $1` semantics across calls.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor is taken from `page[last].created_at` (whatever type the driver returns) and fed back as `$1` against `created_at < $1`. If the driver returns created_at as a string and the default branch uses a JS `Date` object, the two cursor inputs are not the same type, risking inconsistent comparison/serialization at the param boundary. The contract relies on a stable, comparable cursor type that `SELECT *` plus mixed default does not guarantee.

claudeCands.summary: Non-unique created_at timestamps silently drop and duplicate rows, violating the stated 'every order appears on exactly one page' contract. The cursor and the WHERE clause use strict `created_at < $1` with no tiebreaker column. If multiple orders share the same created_at value (common with bulk inserts, batch imports, or coarse timestamp precision), the last row of a page and any sibling rows with the identical timestamp are skipped entirely on the next page (because the next query excludes everything `< cursor`, but rows EQUAL to cursor that weren't yet shown are lost). Conversely, ORDER BY created_at DESC with ties is non-deterministic across queries, so rows with equal timestamps can also appear on two different pages.
claudeCands.evidence: Cursor is `page[page.length-1].created_at` and the filter is `WHERE created_at < $1`. Ties on created_at have no secondary sort key (e.g. id) in ORDER BY and no compound comparison in WHERE. With duplicate timestamps, sibling rows sharing the boundary created_at are neither `< cursor` (so excluded next page) nor were they necessarily returned this page — they fall through the crack. SELECT * also gives no stable secondary order.

claudeCands.summary: The first-page default cursor `new Date()` omits orders created at or after request time and is timezone/clock-skew fragile. Using `created_at < new Date()` (strict less-than) for the initial page silently excludes any order whose created_at equals or exceeds the server's current instant — e.g. future-dated orders, orders inserted concurrently during the request, or rows whose DB timestamp is ahead of the app server's clock. There is no handling of the boundary, and the omission is silent: such orders never appear on any page.
claudeCands.evidence: `[cursor ?? new Date(), limit + 1]` substitutes the app-process wall clock into `created_at < $1`. Any row with created_at >= that instant (future orders, clock skew between app and DB, rows inserted mid-pagination) is permanently excluded from page 1 and, since later cursors only move backward in time, from all subsequent pages too.

claudeCands.summary: No validation or bounds on `limit`, and the cursor's type/value is unchecked. The contract and signature are silent on rejecting non-positive, non-integer, NaN, or absurdly large limits, and on validating that `cursor` is a real date. `limit = 0` makes `LIMIT 1` (returns 1 row but `page` is empty and hasMore is computed wrong), negative limits produce a SQL error or LIMIT -1 semantics, and a huge limit allows unbounded result sets (DoS). A malformed/string cursor is passed straight into the comparison with no parse or rejection.
claudeCands.evidence: `limit = 20` default but no guard before `LIMIT $2` with `limit + 1`; with limit=0, query asks LIMIT 1, `rows.length > 0` may be true so hasMore=true while `rows.slice(0,0)` yields empty page and nextCursor=null — internally inconsistent. `cursor ?? new Date()` only guards null/undefined, not invalid Date or wrong type.

claudeCands.summary: Missing error handling, transactional/read-consistency guarantees, and concurrency semantics for the 'pages are stable' claim. The contract promises stable pages, but there is no snapshot/consistent-read mechanism: orders inserted, deleted, or having created_at mutated between page fetches will shift the result set, so 'stable' is unachievable as written. There is also no try/catch around the awaited db.query — a transient DB error rejects the promise with no defined handling, and no documentation of what the caller should expect.
claudeCands.evidence: Each call is an independent SELECT against live data with no transaction, snapshot isolation, or as-of timestamp. Comment asserts 'pages are stable' but nothing pins a consistent view across the multi-call pagination session. The single `await db.query(...)` has no error handling; rejection propagates uncaught relative to the contract, which says nothing about failure modes.

claudeCands.summary: No way to detect or signal the end of pagination unambiguously, and nextCursor is returned even on the final page. When the last page is reached, `hasMore` is false but `nextCursor` is still set to the created_at of the last row (non-null whenever the page is non-empty). A caller that loops 'until nextCursor is null' instead of checking hasMore will issue one extra query that returns zero rows, and the contract does not specify that hasMore is the authoritative stop condition versus nextCursor.
claudeCands.evidence: `nextCursor = page.length ? page[page.length-1].created_at : null` — on the final non-empty page nextCursor is non-null while hasMore is false. The two termination signals (hasMore vs nextCursor===null) disagree on the last page, and the contract/comment never states which is authoritative, inviting an off-by-one extra fetch or premature stop.

claudeCands.summary: SELECT * leaks all columns and couples the API to schema changes; no column projection or field allowlist is specified. The function returns whole rows verbatim as `orders`, so any sensitive column (internal flags, PII, payment tokens) is exposed to every caller, and adding/renaming a column silently changes the public payload. The contract says nothing about which fields are part of the page shape.
claudeCands.evidence: `SELECT * FROM orders` returns every column; `page` (a slice of raw rows) is returned directly as `orders` with no projection, mapping, or DTO. No field-selection contract is documented despite this being a paginated public-looking list API.

claudeCands.summary: Contract claims 'every order appears on exactly one page' but the strict '<' comparison on a non-unique created_at column silently DROPS rows, violating exactly-once. created_at is not guaranteed unique; multiple orders can share the same timestamp (common with batch inserts or coarse timestamp resolution). The query is `WHERE created_at < $1` (strict less-than) and the next cursor is `page[page.length-1].created_at`. If the boundary timestamp T is shared by several rows and only some land on the current page, the next query `created_at < T` excludes ALL rows with created_at == T, including the ones not yet returned. Those orders appear on ZERO pages. The 'exactly one page' contract is therefore false.
claudeCands.evidence: Query: `WHERE created_at < $1` combined with `nextCursor = page[page.length - 1].created_at`. Example: 21 rows all with created_at = T, limit = 20. Page 1 returns 20 rows; nextCursor = T. Page 2 runs `created_at < T` which returns 0 rows — the 21st order with created_at == T is lost. hasMore was even true (rows.length 21 > 20), so the caller is told to continue, yet the missing row is never returned.

claudeCands.summary: Conversely, the same code can make an order appear on MORE than one page (duplication) — also breaking 'exactly one page'. This is the mirror failure depending on tie handling: because ordering by a non-unique created_at is non-deterministic for ties, the DB may return different subsets/orders of the tied rows across the two queries. With strict '<' a tied row at the boundary is dropped (loss); but the contract's 'exactly one' is two-sided and the comment offers no tie-breaker (e.g., a secondary key like id) to make the boundary deterministic, so neither at-least-once nor at-most-once is actually guaranteed.
claudeCands.evidence: ORDER BY created_at DESC has no secondary sort key. SQL does not guarantee a stable/deterministic order among rows with equal created_at, so the set of rows with created_at == nextCursor that were 'already seen' vs. 'not yet seen' is undefined. The code's only boundary mechanism is the timestamp value, which cannot distinguish tied rows — making the 'exactly one page' claim unprovable and false in the tie case.

claudeCands.summary: Contract claims 'pages are stable' but the code provides no snapshot/consistency mechanism, so concurrent inserts and deletes shift page contents. 'Stable pages' would require that a given cursor always yields the same rows. Because the query reads live data with no high-watermark/snapshot isolation, an order inserted with a created_at between two page fetches, or a deletion, changes what each cursor returns. While cursor pagination on created_at is more stable than OFFSET, inserts of OLD-dated rows (backfills, imports, clock skew) below the current cursor will appear on later pages and shift the boundary — the absolute 'pages are stable' claim is unqualified and overstated.
claudeCands.evidence: No `SELECT ... AS OF`, snapshot transaction, or fixed upper-bound watermark. Each call independently runs `WHERE created_at < $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC`. An insert with created_at < cursor (backdated order, replication lag, non-monotonic clock) between fetches lands in a not-yet-read region and alters subsequent page contents, contradicting unqualified 'stable'.

claudeCands.summary: The initial-page default `cursor ?? new Date()` is claimed to start the newest-first listing, but it silently EXCLUDES the newest orders due to strict '<' against a client/server 'now' that may be earlier than rows' created_at. If any order has created_at >= the JS `new Date()` value (future-dated orders, clock skew between app server and DB, or DB-side created_at set by a slightly-ahead DB clock / DEFAULT now()), those rows satisfy neither `created_at < now`, so the very first page omits them — they appear on no page, again breaking 'exactly one page'. The comment frames the cursor purely as 'created_at of the last row seen' and gives no account of this initial-boundary hazard.
claudeCands.evidence: `[cursor ?? new Date(), ...]` feeds a JS-side timestamp into `created_at < $1`. JS `new Date()` (app server clock) is compared against DB `created_at`. Any row with created_at >= that instant (clock skew, future-dated, or rows created during the request) is permanently excluded from the first page and never reachable via subsequent (older) cursors.

claudeCands.summary: The `hasMore` flag is computed correctly for the limit+1 probe, but the comment/contract implies it reliably signals 'more orders exist'; in the tie-at-boundary loss scenario hasMore can be true while the next page returns empty, making hasMore an overclaimed 'has more reachable pages' signal. A caller trusting hasMore===true to mean 'a non-empty next page exists' will loop into an empty result, or worse, treat the empty page as end-of-data after being told more existed — an inconsistency the contract does not acknowledge.
claudeCands.evidence: `hasMore = rows.length > limit` is derived from the +1 probe row, but that probe row may itself be a tied-timestamp row that the next `created_at < nextCursor` query drops. So hasMore=true does not imply the next cursor yields any rows — the 'has more' meaning is stronger than what the strict-'<' cursor can deliver.

claudeCands.summary: SELECT * fetches every column on every page, inflating row width, network transfer, and DB I/O cost — the function returns whole rows but pagination only needs the displayed fields plus the cursor key (created_at). On a wide orders table (JSON blobs, addresses, line-item snapshots) this multiplies bytes-scanned and bytes-shipped per page with no benefit.
claudeCands.evidence: Line: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at < $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT $2. The only field the function logically depends on is page[...].created_at for nextCursor; the rest is passed through verbatim. Projecting only needed columns (or at minimum excluding large/unused columns) cuts per-page cost proportionally to row width.

claudeCands.summary: Cursor on a non-unique created_at column breaks the stated contract AND causes either skipped or duplicated rows — a correctness defect with direct cost consequences: clients re-paginate or reconcile, and duplicate rows mean extra downstream processing/transfer that is pure waste. Two orders with identical created_at straddling a page boundary: the strict `<` cursor skips all remaining rows sharing that timestamp on the next page.
claudeCands.evidence: WHERE created_at < $1 with cursor = page[last].created_at. If rows N and N+1 share the same created_at and the page ends at N, the next query excludes N+1 (and all same-timestamp peers) because the predicate is strictly `< created_at`. This violates 'every order appears on exactly one page' — some appear zero times. A correct keyset cursor must be a composite (created_at, id) tuple compared lexicographically, which also affects index/cost design.

claudeCands.summary: Default cursor `new Date()` (client/server clock 'now') silently drops any order whose created_at is >= that instant, and is non-deterministic across calls — first page is not stable. Rows inserted between two first-page requests, or rows with future-dated created_at, are invisible, forcing callers to add their own scans/reconciliation. The contract claims 'pages are stable' but the entry point is time-dependent.
claudeCands.evidence: cursor ?? new Date() in params, used as created_at < $1. Each invocation without a cursor computes a fresh wall-clock bound, so the first page boundary moves; 'pages are stable' is false at the head. A stable approach uses an open first page (no upper bound, or a fixed snapshot cursor) rather than re-evaluating new Date() per call.

claudeCands.summary: No index is guaranteed/declared on created_at, so ORDER BY created_at DESC + WHERE created_at < $1 can degrade to a full-table scan + filesort — the dominant cost risk for keyset pagination. Without a matching descending-compatible index, every page does O(table) work instead of O(limit), and cost grows unbounded with table size even though only 21 rows are returned.
claudeCands.evidence: Query relies on an ordered range scan: WHERE created_at < $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT limit+1. This is only cheap with an index on (created_at) (ideally (created_at, id) to support the tie-break cursor). The snippet/contract documents none; if absent, Postgres sorts the full filtered set per page — the classic cost trap this pagination style is supposed to avoid.

claudeCands.summary: Returning hasMore AND nextCursor is redundant payload, and nextCursor is computed even when hasMore is false — minor but it means callers may issue one extra empty 'tail' page request (a full round-trip + query for zero results) if they paginate on nextCursor!=null rather than hasMore. The last page still has a non-null nextCursor, inviting a wasted final query.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor = page.length ? page[last].created_at : null — non-null on the final non-empty page even though hasMore is false. A client looping `while (nextCursor)` will make one extra query that returns nothing. The cost-safe contract is to set nextCursor = hasMore ? page[last].created_at : null so the cursor itself signals end-of-data and no tail request is made.

claudeCands.summary: Non-unique cursor on duplicate created_at causes rows to be SKIPPED (lost) across pages, violating the 'every order appears on exactly one page' contract. This is the central deep-reasoning flaw.
claudeCands.evidence: The cursor is `created_at` alone and the WHERE clause is strict `created_at < $1`. If multiple orders share the same `created_at` timestamp (extremely common: bulk inserts, batch jobs, low-resolution timestamps, same-second creation), the page boundary can fall in the middle of a group of ties. Suppose 25 rows have created_at = T and limit = 20. Page 1 returns rows ending at created_at = T (nextCursor = T). Page 2 queries `created_at < T`, which EXCLUDES all remaining rows that also equal T. Those ~5 tied orders are never returned on any page. The contract 'every order appears on exactly one page' is broken. The correct keyset approach requires a composite cursor (created_at, id) with a tuple comparison `(created_at, id) < ($1, $2)` and a deterministic tie-break in ORDER BY (created_at DESC, id DESC).

claudeCands.summary: ORDER BY created_at DESC is not a total order — without a unique tie-breaker column, the sort among rows with equal created_at is non-deterministic, breaking the 'pages are stable' contract and making hasMore/boundary decisions unreliable.
claudeCands.evidence: ORDER BY created_at DESC leaves ties in arbitrary, engine-dependent order (no guaranteed stable sort in SQL). The same query run twice can return tied rows in different orders, so the row chosen as the page boundary (page[length-1]) can differ between requests. This directly violates 'pages are stable' and compounds the skip/duplicate problem above. Fix: add a unique secondary sort key, e.g. ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC.

claudeCands.summary: First-page default cursor `new Date()` uses strict `<`, silently dropping any order whose created_at is >= the call instant (future-dated rows or same-millisecond inserts), so the newest rows can be excluded from page 1.
claudeCands.evidence: `cursor ?? new Date()` defaults the first page to the current wall-clock time, then filters `created_at < $1`. Any order with created_at exactly equal to or after that captured instant is excluded. Future-dated orders (scheduled orders, clock skew between app server and DB, timezone/`now()` mismatches) and rows inserted in the same millisecond as the call will never appear. A correct newest-first first page should have no upper bound at all (omit the WHERE on first page) or use a sentinel that includes the max possible timestamp.

claudeCands.summary: Cursor type/serialization mismatch: nextCursor is returned as a JS Date (or DB timestamp) but is consumed back as $1 with no normalization; round-tripping through JSON/HTTP turns it into a string and can lose sub-second precision, shifting the boundary and causing skips or repeats.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor = page[last].created_at is whatever the driver returns (Date object or timestamp string). When a client sends it back as a query-string/JSON value it becomes an ISO string, which may be truncated to millisecond (or second) precision while Postgres timestamps hold microseconds. Re-parsing `created_at < '2026-06-15T12:00:00.123Z'` against a stored value of ...123456 changes which rows match, so boundary rows can be skipped or duplicated. The function never validates or canonicalizes the incoming cursor.

claudeCands.summary: Concurrent inserts of newer rows are fine, but the design has no snapshot/consistency guard — and more importantly there is no handling for the empty-page nextCursor, which conflicts with hasMore and can mislead callers into stopping early or looping.
claudeCands.evidence: When a page comes back empty (page.length === 0, e.g. cursor older than all rows), nextCursor is null and hasMore is false — acceptable. But consider hasMore=true with a valid nextCursor that lands on a tie boundary: callers trust hasMore to decide whether to fetch again, yet the next fetch may return fewer-than-expected or skip rows (per defect #1). hasMore being true does not guarantee the next page actually surfaces the 'more' rows it implies, so hasMore is not a reliable termination signal in the presence of ties.

claudeCands.summary: `SELECT *` returns all columns including potentially sensitive/large fields and couples the cursor logic to physical schema; combined with no index guarantee on created_at, the query can also be a performance defect (full scan/sort).
claudeCands.evidence: `SELECT *` over `orders` is an anti-pattern for a hot list endpoint: it ships every column (PII, blobs) to the app and breaks if columns are added/reordered. Separately, the query's efficiency depends entirely on an index on `created_at` (ideally a composite `(created_at DESC, id DESC)` to match the corrected keyset). Nothing in the artifact establishes such an index; without it, `ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 21` degrades to a sort of the whole table as data grows. This is both a security/coupling and a scalability defect.

claudeCands.summary: No validation/clamping of `limit` — a caller passing 0, a negative, NaN, or a huge value produces incorrect hasMore math or unbounded result sets.
claudeCands.evidence: `limit + 1` and `rows.length > limit` assume limit is a sane positive integer. limit=0 → query LIMIT 1, page=slice(0,0)=[], nextCursor=null, but hasMore = (1>0)=true: an empty page that claims more rows, a contradiction. Negative limit → LIMIT (negative) errors or behaves driver-specifically. A huge limit lets a single call pull the entire table (DoS). The function should clamp limit to [1, MAX] and coerce/validate to integer.

claudeCands.summary: Cursor (created_at < $1) and ORDER BY assume monotonic, immutable created_at; if created_at is ever updated/backfilled or if rows are inserted with out-of-order timestamps, previously-paged rows can reappear or be skipped — the stability contract has no enforcement.
claudeCands.evidence: Keyset pagination on created_at is only stable if created_at is set-once and never moves. Backfills, data migrations, or 'touch' operations that rewrite created_at will shift a row across a boundary that has already been cursored past, causing it to be seen twice (if moved older) or never (if moved newer relative to an already-issued cursor). The contract 'every order appears on exactly one page' is asserted but nothing in the code or query enforces immutability or detects such drift.

claudeCands.summary: Non-unique created_at at a page boundary silently DROPS rows, breaking the 'every order on exactly one page' contract (primary correctness bug).
claudeCands.evidence: Predicate is strict 'created_at < $1' and the cursor is created_at. If rows share a timestamp across the boundary, they vanish. Trace (limit=2, timestamps DESC [10,9,9,9,8]): Page1 fetches LIMIT 3 -> [10,9,9], page=[10,9], nextCursor=9; Page2 queries created_at < 9 -> [8]. The third row with ts=9 is never returned on any page. Confirmed by simulation.

claudeCands.summary: No unique tiebreaker in ORDER BY causes duplicates and unstable pages among equal timestamps, violating 'pages are stable'.
claudeCands.evidence: 'ORDER BY created_at DESC' has no secondary key. Postgres may order rows with equal created_at differently between the two queries (plan/concurrency/heap changes). If the relative order of tied rows flips, a boundary row can reappear at the top of the next region (duplicate), and re-running the same query can yield different page contents (instability). A correct keyset needs a compound cursor, e.g. '(created_at, id) < ($1, $2)' ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC.

claudeCands.summary: First-page default 'cursor ?? new Date()' permanently hides the newest orders and makes page 1 non-deterministic.
claudeCands.evidence: On first call cursor is undefined, so the boundary is wall-clock now. Any order with created_at >= now (clock skew, future-dated rows, or same-millisecond inserts) is excluded from page 1; since cursors only move backward in time, those rows are excluded from every page. Page-1 content also depends on the server clock rather than being a stable snapshot.

claudeCands.summary: nextCursor is returned non-null even when hasMore is false; the two pagination signals disagree.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor = page.length ? page[last].created_at : null, so it is non-null on any non-empty final page even though hasMore===false. A caller looping 'while nextCursor !== null' makes an extra zero-row query, and a caller trusting nextCursor over hasMore believes more data exists. nextCursor should be null when hasMore is false.

claudeCands.summary: Timestamp precision loss on cursor round-trip (Postgres microseconds vs JS Date / ISO milliseconds) can re-skip or duplicate boundary rows.
claudeCands.evidence: Postgres timestamp has microsecond precision; new Date() and JSON/ISO serialization have only millisecond precision. Demonstrated: DB '2026-06-15 12:00:00.123456' -> JS toISOString '...123Z', the .456us is lost. A nextCursor derived from a microsecond timestamp, serialized to the client and sent back, no longer compares equal/strict-less correctly at the boundary under 'created_at < $1', dropping or repeating sub-millisecond-adjacent rows.

claudeCands.summary: limit is unvalidated; limit=0 (and negative) yields hasMore=true with nextCursor=null and an empty page, stranding the caller.
claudeCands.evidence: Simulation with limit=0 and 5 rows: fetch LIMIT 1 -> rows.length 1, hasMore = 1 > 0 = true, page = slice(0,0) = [], nextCursor = null. Caller is told more exists but gets zero rows and no cursor to advance -> stuck/infinite loop. Negative limit gives slice with negative end (drops from the end) with the same stranded result. Huge/non-integer limit is also unbounded (memory/DoS). limit needs clamping to a sane integer range.

claudeCands.summary: The stated absolute contract ('every order appears on exactly one page; pages are stable') is unachievable under concurrent inserts/deletes with this design and is overstated.
claudeCands.evidence: Keyset pagination over a live table cannot guarantee an absolute one-page-per-row, fully-stable contract: an order inserted with created_at > cursor after page 1 is fetched will never appear, and deletions shift counts. Even setting ties aside, the implementation only provides stability for the already-traversed region relative to the moving cursor, not the global invariant the comment claims.

claudeCands.summary: SELECT * couples the endpoint to physical schema, over-fetches, and can leak columns not intended for this API.
claudeCands.evidence: 'SELECT * FROM orders' returns every column (including potentially large or sensitive ones) and is sensitive to column add/reorder. An explicit column list scoped to what the order list needs is required for stability and to avoid leaking internal fields.

claudeCands.summary: Non-unique cursor breaks the 'exactly one page' contract: ties on created_at are silently dropped or duplicated. The WHERE clause uses strict 'created_at < $1' where $1 is the last row's created_at. If multiple orders share the same created_at timestamp and a page boundary lands among them, all rows with a created_at equal to the cursor are EXCLUDED from the next page (because '<' is strict, not '<='). Those tied rows that didn't fit on the current page are skipped entirely — violating 'every order appears on exactly one page'.
claudeCands.evidence: WHERE created_at < $1 with $1 = page[page.length-1].created_at. Example: 25 rows all with created_at = T, limit = 20. Page 1 returns 20 rows (cursor = T). Page 2 queries created_at < T, which excludes the remaining 5 rows with created_at = T. They are permanently lost. created_at is not a unique key (it's a timestamp), so collisions are realistic, especially under bulk inserts or coarse timestamp resolution.

claudeCands.summary: No tiebreaker in ORDER BY makes row order non-deterministic across queries, so the keyset boundary itself is unstable. ORDER BY created_at DESC alone does not define a total order when timestamps collide; the DB may return tied rows in different relative orders between the two queries that share a boundary. Combined with the strict '<' cursor, this can both drop rows and (with '<=') duplicate them. Pages are therefore not 'stable' as the contract requires.
claudeCands.evidence: ORDER BY created_at DESC has no secondary key (e.g., ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC). Keyset/seek pagination requires the ORDER BY columns to form a unique total order; otherwise the cursor cannot unambiguously identify a position. The contract explicitly promises 'pages are stable.'

claudeCands.summary: First-page default cursor 'new Date()' silently filters out future-dated and clock-skew rows, and uses app-server time instead of DB time. When cursor is null/undefined, it defaults to JS new Date() and the query becomes created_at < now(). Any order with created_at >= the app server's current time (future-dated orders, scheduled orders, or rows inserted by a DB whose clock is ahead, or app-server clock skew behind the DB) will never appear on page 1 — or on any page — violating 'every order appears on exactly one page.'
claudeCands.evidence: [cursor ?? new Date(), ...]. The default should be an open first page (omit the WHERE predicate when no cursor), not 'created_at < app_server_now'. Mixing application-process wall-clock time with database timestamps is a classic source of dropped rows under clock skew; the comparison value should come from the DB or the predicate should be absent on the first page.

claudeCands.summary: Concurrent inserts during pagination cause newer orders to be missed (acceptable for newest-first) but also cause the 'exactly one page' invariant to fail for boundary-time inserts. Because the cursor is created_at and new rows are inserted with created_at between page fetches, a row inserted with created_at less than the current cursor but after page 1 was read is fine; however a row inserted with created_at exactly equal to an existing boundary timestamp interacts with the strict '<' bug above. More fundamentally, the snapshot is not consistent across page requests, so 'every order appears on exactly one page' cannot hold for a mutating table.
claudeCands.evidence: Pagination spans multiple independent queries with no snapshot/MVCC isolation guarantee across calls. The contract is an absolute invariant ('exactly one page') that is unachievable for a live-mutating table without a stable snapshot or an immutable, unique, monotonic cursor key — created_at is neither unique nor guaranteed monotonic.

claudeCands.summary: nextCursor is returned as non-null on the final page (when hasMore is false), so a caller that loops 'while nextCursor != null' will issue one extra, redundant query and may loop indefinitely if it doesn't also check hasMore. On the last page, page is non-empty but there are no more rows; yet nextCursor is set to the last row's created_at instead of null. The signal for 'stop' is split inconsistently between nextCursor and hasMore.
claudeCands.evidence: const nextCursor = page.length ? page[page.length-1].created_at : null; — this is independent of hasMore. When hasMore===false and page.length>0, nextCursor is still a timestamp. A client paginating on nextCursor alone never terminates cleanly; it must use hasMore. nextCursor should be null when !hasMore.

claudeCands.summary: SELECT * is used, which is fragile and leaks schema: column additions/reordering change the payload, and it forces fetching every column when only created_at is needed for the cursor. This is both an efficiency and a stability defect for an API contract.
claudeCands.evidence: SELECT * FROM orders. For a public paginated API promising stable behavior, explicit column lists are required; SELECT * couples the response shape to physical table layout.

claudeCands.summary: No validation/clamping of limit: a caller passing a huge or non-positive limit causes unbounded result sets or a LIMIT 0/negative. limit defaults to 20 only when undefined; an explicit limit of 0 yields LIMIT 1 (limit+1) returning at most 1 row with hasMore math off, and a negative limit (e.g., -1) yields LIMIT 0 returning no rows and page.length 0 → nextCursor null even though more data exists, silently terminating pagination and dropping all remaining orders.
claudeCands.evidence: limit = 20 default only applies to undefined; LIMIT $2 with [.. , limit+1]. limit=-1 → LIMIT 0 → rows=[], hasMore=false, page=[], nextCursor=null. limit=0 → LIMIT 1, hasMore = (1>0)=true always-ish, page=rows.slice(0,0)=[] so nextCursor=null but hasMore=true — contradictory signals. No upper bound allows a client to request LIMIT 1e9.

claudeCands.summary: hasMore can be a false positive at an exact tie boundary, reporting more pages when the extra (limit+1)th row is a duplicate-timestamp row that will be excluded by the next query's strict '<'. The over-fetch-by-one heuristic assumes the (limit+1)th row will actually be returned by the next page query, but with the strict '<' cursor and tied timestamps it will be filtered out, so hasMore=true yet the next page is empty (or skips it).
claudeCands.evidence: hasMore = rows.length > limit; combined with WHERE created_at < cursor. The +1 probe row at index 20 may share created_at with page[19]; next query excludes everything == cursor, so the probed row is dropped and hasMore was misleading.

claudeCands.summary: Timestamp serialization round-trip on cursor: the returned nextCursor is a JS Date / DB timestamp object that the client must echo back; precision loss (e.g., millisecond truncation, timezone normalization, or JSON string round-tripping losing sub-second/microsecond precision) shifts the boundary and can skip or repeat rows. Postgres timestamps carry microsecond precision that a JS Date (millisecond) cannot represent, so re-sending the cursor as a Date loses microseconds.
claudeCands.evidence: nextCursor = page[...].created_at flows out and back in as cursor with no canonical encoding. Postgres timestamp microsecond resolution > JS Date millisecond resolution; rounding the cursor changes which rows satisfy 'created_at < $1', breaking the exactly-once invariant at sub-millisecond boundaries.

claudeCands.summary: created_at is assumed monotonically assigned and immutable, but if any order's created_at can be updated (backfills, corrections) or if created_at is set by application code (not DB default), the keyset ordering shifts mid-pagination, relocating rows across page boundaries and breaking stability.
claudeCands.evidence: The entire cursor scheme depends on created_at being immutable and total-orderable. Nothing enforces this; the comment merely asserts 'Cursor = created_at of the last row seen' without guaranteeing uniqueness or immutability.

planted_defects.defect: Contradiction (algorithm confusion): trusts header.alg from the token; alg=HS256/none lets the RSA PUBLIC_KEY be used as an HMAC secret, so a forged token verifies. Expected alg must be pinned.
Omission: no exp/nbf (expiry) or iss/aud validation, so expired or wrong-audience tokens authenticate.
Deep-reasoning: split('.') doesn't guard structure; missing/extra segments or alg='none' (empty sig) yield library-dependent crypto.verify behavior that can bypass verification.

claudeCands.summary: Algorithm confusion / unauthenticated alg field: header.alg is taken from the attacker-controlled token and passed directly into crypto.verify, so the verifier does not pin the expected algorithm. An attacker can set alg to whatever they like. Critically, since PUBLIC_KEY is an RSA public key (which is, by definition, public), an attacker can switch alg to an HMAC variant (e.g. HS256) and forge a token by HMAC-signing it with the public key bytes as the HMAC secret. The verify call would then succeed against an attacker-forged signature. The algorithm MUST be hardcoded/whitelisted by the verifier, never read from the token header.
claudeCands.evidence: const ok = crypto.verify(header.alg, Buffer.from(h + '.' + p), PUBLIC_KEY, b64urlDecode(sig)); — alg comes from JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(h)) (attacker input), not from a server-side constant. With a public RSA key this enables the classic RS256->HS256 algorithm-confusion forgery.

claudeCands.summary: 'none' algorithm bypass: because alg is unvalidated, an attacker can set alg to 'none' (or any value Node's crypto treats as no-signature / a digest-only mode). Depending on how crypto.verify handles an unrecognized or 'none' algorithm, this either throws (DoS) or, worse, can lead to acceptance paths. The verifier must reject any token whose alg is not the single expected asymmetric algorithm before calling verify.
claudeCands.evidence: header.alg is passed straight to crypto.verify with no check such as `if (header.alg !== 'RS256') throw`. The classic JWT 'alg: none' bypass is unguarded.

claudeCands.summary: No expiration / claims validation: the function returns payload as 'trusted' but never checks exp, nbf, iat, iss, or aud. A token that is correctly signed but expired (or not yet valid, or issued for a different audience/issuer) is accepted indefinitely. For something 'used to authenticate API requests' this means revocation-by-expiry does not work and tokens are effectively eternal once signed.
claudeCands.evidence: return payload; // token is now trusted — there is no `if (payload.exp && Date.now()/1000 > payload.exp) throw` nor any aud/iss check anywhere in the function.

claudeCands.summary: Malformed token shape not validated: token.split('.') is destructured into [h, p, sig] with no check that exactly three non-empty parts exist. A token with too few parts (e.g. 'abc' or 'a.b') leaves p and/or sig as undefined; b64urlDecode(undefined) / JSON.parse on the result will throw a generic error rather than a controlled 'invalid token'. A token with extra dots ('a.b.c.d') silently drops the 4th segment. The signed message is reconstructed as h + '.' + p, so any structural mismatch is not safely rejected.
claudeCands.evidence: const [h, p, sig] = token.split('.'); with no `if (parts.length !== 3) throw`. sig may be undefined -> b64urlDecode(sig) behavior is undefined; extra segments past index 2 are silently ignored.

claudeCands.summary: Signed bytes vs. verified bytes mismatch risk (canonicalization): the signing input is reconstructed as Buffer.from(h + '.' + p) using the raw received segments, which is correct ONLY if the signer signed the exact same base64url encoding. But JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(p)) is computed and returned independently of signature verification — the returned payload object is derived from decoding, while trust is asserted on the raw bytes. If b64urlDecode is lenient (accepts non-canonical base64, padding variants, or whitespace), two distinct h/p strings could decode to the same bytes, and the trusted object may not byte-for-byte correspond to what a strict signer intended. Decoding should happen only after a successful verify, and base64url decoding must be strict/canonical.
claudeCands.evidence: header/payload are JSON.parse-decoded BEFORE `if (!ok)`, and the verified message uses raw `h + '.' + p` while the returned value uses decoded bytes — no guarantee b64urlDecode is canonical/strict.

claudeCands.summary: Buffer.from default encoding bug: Buffer.from(h + '.' + p) with no encoding argument defaults to UTF-8 of the string. That is actually what a JWT signs (the ASCII signing input), so this is correct for ASCII — but it is fragile and undocumented intent; more importantly there is no MIME/charset pinning. The real latent defect is that the message passed to verify is a JS string-derived buffer while sig is decoded bytes; if the platform's crypto.verify for the chosen RSA scheme expects a pre-hashed digest vs. raw message, mismatch produces silent verify=false (auth outage) rather than an error. Worth flagging as an integration-correctness hazard.
claudeCands.evidence: Buffer.from(h + '.' + p) — relies on implicit UTF-8 and on crypto.verify(algorithm,...) hashing internally; no explicit encoding and no test that the signer's hash/padding scheme matches.

claudeCands.summary: Exceptions leak / inconsistent failure mode: JSON.parse and b64urlDecode can throw raw SyntaxError/range errors before the signature check, so a caller catching only 'bad signature' will get different, uncaught exception types for malformed input. All rejection paths should funnel to a single uniform 'invalid token' to avoid information leakage and inconsistent handling, and to avoid an attacker distinguishing 'malformed' from 'bad signature' via error type/timing.
claudeCands.evidence: const header = JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(h)); runs before `if (!ok) throw new Error('bad signature')` — parse/decode throw their own error types, so failure modes are not uniform; only the signature path throws the controlled message.

claudeCands.summary: Algorithm confusion / attacker-controlled algorithm: the verification uses header.alg (taken from the untrusted token itself) to select the signature algorithm. An attacker controls the JWT header, so they choose which algorithm is used to verify. Combined with crypto.verify accepting the algorithm name from the token, this enables classic JWT algorithm-confusion attacks (e.g., forcing a different/weaker algorithm) — the algorithm must be pinned by the server, not read from the token.
claudeCands.evidence: const header = JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(h)); ... crypto.verify(header.alg, Buffer.from(h + '.' + p), PUBLIC_KEY, b64urlDecode(sig)); — the algorithm passed to crypto.verify is header.alg, which comes directly from the untrusted token. There is no allow-list check (e.g., if (header.alg !== 'RS256') throw).

claudeCands.summary: RSA-vs-HMAC key confusion (RS256 -> HS256 forgery): because alg is attacker-controlled AND the RSA PUBLIC_KEY is passed to crypto.verify, an attacker can set header.alg to an HMAC algorithm (HS256). Node's crypto.verify with a symmetric algorithm name treats the supplied key bytes as the HMAC secret. Since the RSA public key is, by definition, public, the attacker can compute a valid HMAC over the header+payload using the public key as the secret and forge a fully valid token.
claudeCands.evidence: crypto.verify(header.alg, Buffer.from(h + '.' + p), PUBLIC_KEY, b64urlDecode(sig)) — PUBLIC_KEY (publicly known RSA key) is used as the verification key for whatever algorithm header.alg names. With alg='HS256' the public key becomes a known HMAC secret, enabling forgery. Comment confirms PUBLIC_KEY is the RSA public key.

claudeCands.summary: 'none' / empty algorithm bypass not blocked: nothing rejects alg='none' or otherwise unexpected algorithm values before calling crypto.verify. Depending on how crypto.verify and b64urlDecode handle an empty/absent signature and a 'none' algorithm, this is the canonical JWT 'alg:none' authentication bypass. There is no validation that a signature is required.
claudeCands.evidence: No guard on header.alg ('none', null, '') and no check that sig is non-empty before crypto.verify(header.alg, ..., b64urlDecode(sig)). sig could be the empty string if the token ends with a trailing dot.

claudeCands.summary: No validation of token structure before destructuring/parsing — malformed token causes uncontrolled behavior. token.split('.') is not checked for exactly 3 parts. A token with fewer parts yields undefined for h/p/sig; b64urlDecode(undefined)/JSON.parse may throw raw errors (DoS / information disclosure via stack traces) or, worse, behave unexpectedly. A non-string/empty token also throws on .split.
claudeCands.evidence: const [h, p, sig] = token.split('.'); — no length check (expected 3 segments). h, p, or sig can be undefined; JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(h)) then runs on undefined/garbage. No try/catch around parsing.

claudeCands.summary: No verification of token claims after signature check — a structurally valid, correctly signed token is returned as 'trusted' with zero claim validation. Missing checks for expiration (exp), not-before (nbf), issuer (iss), audience (aud), and subject. A leaked/old token never expires from this code's perspective, and a token signed by the same key for a different service/audience is accepted, enabling replay and cross-service token reuse.
claudeCands.evidence: if (!ok) throw new Error('bad signature'); return payload; // token is now trusted — returns immediately after signature verification with no exp/nbf/iss/aud checks. The trailing comment explicitly treats it as fully trusted.

claudeCands.summary: Signature verification result handling is non-strict — relies solely on crypto.verify returning a truthy boolean. If header.alg is an algorithm crypto.verify does not recognize or that causes it to throw/return a non-boolean, the falsy-only check may misbehave; more importantly there is no defense-in-depth (e.g., constant-time, key-type pinning). The single boolean 'ok' is the entire trust boundary with no surrounding error handling, so any exception path (parse error, unknown alg) escapes uncaught.
claudeCands.evidence: const ok = crypto.verify(header.alg, ...); if (!ok) throw new Error('bad signature'); — the only gate. crypto.verify can throw for unsupported algorithm/key combinations, and there is no try/catch, so the failure mode for attacker-chosen alg values is undefined rather than a clean rejection.

claudeCands.summary: Malformed/non-string tokens cause uncaught exceptions instead of a clean authentication failure (DoS / error-handling bypass). The function never validates the shape of `token` before destructuring.
claudeCands.evidence: Empirically verified on Node v20.19.6. `token.split('.')` throws TypeError for non-string input: undefined -> "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'split')", null -> same, number 12345 -> "token.split is not a function or its return value is not iterable". For short tokens, split yields undefined parts: "onlyheader".split('.') => {h:'onlyheader', p:undefined, sig:undefined}, and "".split('.') => {h:''}. Then `JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(undefined))` and `JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(''))` both throw SyntaxError ("Unexpected end of JSON input"). A bad-base64 header throws SyntaxError too. None of these are caught, so a single malformed request crashes the verify path / leaks a stack trace instead of returning 'bad signature'.

claudeCands.summary: Algorithm is taken from the attacker-controlled, unverified header (`header.alg`) with no allowlist/pinning, enabling algorithm-agility/downgrade attacks. The function trusts the token to declare how it should be verified.
claudeCands.evidence: `crypto.verify(header.alg, ...)` reads alg from JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(h)) which is parsed and used BEFORE any signature check. Verified that the same RSA public key validates multiple algorithms chosen only by the header: crypto.verify('RSA-SHA1', si, publicKey, sigSignedWithSHA1) => true AND crypto.verify('RSA-SHA256', ...) => true. Since nothing pins the expected algorithm, an attacker who can obtain or induce a signature under a weaker/different digest (e.g. RSA-SHA1) can present a header claiming that alg and have it accepted. The correct pattern is to ignore header.alg and pin a single expected algorithm constant.

claudeCands.summary: Certain attacker-chosen `alg` values throw an uncaught 'Invalid digest' exception rather than failing closed, giving a crash/DoS and inconsistent failure behavior.
claudeCands.evidence: Verified: crypto.verify('none', ...) throws "Invalid digest: none"; crypto.verify('MD5-but-fake', ...) throws "Invalid digest: MD5-but-fake". These exceptions are not caught by the function (only the !ok branch throws a controlled 'bad signature'). By contrast alg=undefined and alg=null return false (fail closed). So an attacker setting header.alg='none' (the canonical JWT bypass string) produces an uncaught throw instead of a clean rejection — different, unhandled code path.

claudeCands.summary: Extra '.'-delimited segments beyond three are silently dropped; only the first three are used and only h+'.'+p is signature-covered, so trailing token data is unauthenticated and ignored.
claudeCands.evidence: Verified: 'a.b.c.d'.split('.') => ['a','b','c','d'] but the destructure `const [h,p,sig]` binds only h='a', p='b', sig='c' and discards 'd'. A trailing dot 'a.b.'.split('.') => ['a','b',''] yields an empty-string sig (b64urlDecode('') = empty buffer; crypto.verify(..., Buffer.alloc(0)) returns false, verified). The signed input is constructed as `h + '.' + p`, so any 4th+ segment is never part of the verification and is silently accepted/ignored, masking malformed input rather than rejecting it.

claudeCands.summary: No claims validation (exp/nbf/iat/iss/aud/typ) before returning payload as 'trusted', so expired or wrong-audience tokens with a valid signature are accepted.
claudeCands.evidence: The function returns `payload` immediately after the signature check with the comment 'token is now trusted', and is documented as used to authenticate API requests. There is no check of expiry (exp), not-before (nbf), issuer (iss), audience (aud), or token type (header.typ). A correctly-signed but expired or cross-service token therefore passes verifyToken unconditionally — a signature-valid token is treated as fully authenticated regardless of temporal or scope claims.

claudeCands.summary: Algorithm confusion / 'alg' taken from the untrusted token enables signature bypass — contradicts the stated trust model.
claudeCands.evidence: crypto.verify(header.alg, ...) reads the algorithm name from `header`, which is decoded from the attacker-supplied token BEFORE any signature check. The comment says PUBLIC_KEY is 'our RSA public key' and the token is only 'trusted' after `if (!ok) throw`. But an attacker controls `header.alg`. Two classic breaks follow: (1) Set alg to 'none' / an empty/HMAC-style algorithm and the verify can succeed trivially or be coerced, defeating the 'bad signature' gate. (2) Set alg to an HMAC variant (e.g. HS256); some crypto.verify paths will then treat the RSA *public* key bytes as an HMAC secret — the public key is, by definition, known to the attacker, so they can forge a valid MAC. The code never pins the expected algorithm to RSA, so the security claim in the comment ('PUBLIC_KEY is our RSA public key', token 'now trusted') is inconsistent with what the code actually enforces.

claudeCands.summary: `crypto.verify` is called with the wrong/incomplete argument shape for Node's API — the call as written does not perform the RSA verification the comment claims.
claudeCands.evidence: Node's crypto.verify signature is crypto.verify(algorithm, data, key, signature) where `algorithm` is a hash/digest name like 'sha256' (or null when the key object encodes it), NOT a JWT `alg` value like 'RS256'/'none'. Here `header.alg` (a JWT alg string) is passed as the digest algorithm, and `data` is `Buffer.from(h + '.' + p)` (UTF-8 of the signing input, which is fine), but passing a JWT alg name as the digest will either throw ('Digest method not supported') or silently mis-verify depending on input. So the function's behavior contradicts its own comment that it verifies an RSA signature: with a JWT alg string it does not map to a valid RSA-SHA verification call.

claudeCands.summary: No structural validation of the 3-part split — malformed tokens cause uncaught TypeErrors instead of the intended 'bad signature' rejection, an inconsistent failure mode for an auth gate.
claudeCands.evidence: `const [h, p, sig] = token.split('.')` does not check that exactly 3 parts exist. A token with fewer than 3 segments leaves p and/or sig as `undefined`; b64urlDecode(undefined)/JSON.parse(...) or Buffer.from(undefined) then throws a TypeError, not the 'bad signature' Error the code is designed to raise. The function thus has two distinct rejection paths (thrown TypeError vs. thrown 'bad signature') for what should be a single 'invalid token' outcome — and if any caller distinguishes these or only catches the explicit Error, the behavior is inconsistent with the stated 'authenticate API requests' contract.

claudeCands.summary: Verification result `ok` is not constrained to a strict boolean / the early-throw-then-return-payload flow trusts the payload without verifying it matches the signed bytes' claims (no exp/iss/aud), contradicting 'token is now trusted'.
claudeCands.evidence: The comment asserts the returned payload is 'trusted' once the signature passes, but signature validity only proves the bytes were signed by the key holder — it says nothing about token freshness or audience. There is no check of payload.exp (expiry), nbf, iss, or aud. A previously-issued, now-expired, or wrong-audience token signed by the same key passes `ok` and is returned as 'trusted'. For something 'used to authenticate API requests', returning the payload as trusted with zero claim validation is inconsistent with the security guarantee implied by the comment.

claudeCands.summary: No algorithm allowlist — alg is taken from the attacker-controlled header and passed directly to crypto.verify, enabling algorithm-confusion attacks (alg:'none' or RS256→HS256 substitution).
claudeCands.evidence: Line: `const ok = crypto.verify(header.alg, ..., PUBLIC_KEY, ...)`. The comment says PUBLIC_KEY is an RSA public key, but the code never pins the expected algorithm (e.g. enforcing alg === 'RS256'). An attacker controls `header.alg` because the header is decoded from the untrusted token before any verification. If `header.alg` is 'none' (or a value the crypto lib treats as no-op), verification can be bypassed. Worse, the classic JWT attack: if the verifier accepts an HMAC alg (HS256) while using the RSA *public* key as the HMAC secret, an attacker who knows the public key (it's public!) can forge a valid signature. The function omits any check that header.alg is on a trusted allowlist.

claudeCands.summary: No expiration / time-based claim validation — exp, nbf, and iat are never checked, so expired or not-yet-valid tokens authenticate successfully forever.
claudeCands.evidence: The function returns `payload` immediately after signature check with the comment `// token is now trusted`. There is no read of `payload.exp` (expiration), `payload.nbf` (not-before), or `payload.iat`. A token whose signature is valid but whose lifetime has long passed will still authenticate. Since these tokens authenticate API requests, a leaked/old token is valid indefinitely, and revocation via short expiry is impossible.

claudeCands.summary: No issuer (iss) or audience (aud) validation — a validly-signed token minted for a different service/audience by the same key is accepted.
claudeCands.evidence: Nothing in the function inspects `payload.iss` or `payload.aud`. If the same RSA key (or a key in the same trust domain) signs tokens for multiple services/audiences, a token issued for service B is accepted by this verifier for service A (token-confusion / audience-confusion). The omission means the function trusts any signature from the key regardless of intended recipient or issuer.

claudeCands.summary: Malformed-token handling is omitted — split, b64urlDecode, and JSON.parse can throw or yield undefined on malformed input, producing uncaught exceptions instead of a clean auth failure.
claudeCands.evidence: `token.split('.')` on a token without two dots yields undefined for `p`/`sig`; `b64urlDecode(undefined)` and `JSON.parse(...)` then throw a generic SyntaxError/TypeError rather than the intended `throw new Error('bad signature')`. There is no try/catch and no arity check (e.g. verifying the array has exactly 3 non-empty parts). If `token` itself is null/undefined (missing Authorization header), `token.split` throws TypeError. These uncaught errors can leak stack traces, cause 500s, or — depending on the caller's catch logic — be mishandled as something other than authentication denial.

claudeCands.summary: Verification error from crypto.verify is not distinguished — crypto.verify can throw (bad key, unsupported alg, malformed signature buffer) rather than return false, and that throw path is unhandled.
claudeCands.evidence: `crypto.verify(header.alg, ...)` is treated as if it always returns a boolean `ok`. In Node's crypto API, an unrecognized/invalid `algorithm` argument (again, attacker-controlled via header.alg) or a malformed signature can raise, bypassing the `if (!ok) throw new Error('bad signature')` path with a different, uncaught error. The function omits wrapping the verify call so that ALL failure modes converge on a single 'authentication failed' outcome.

claudeCands.summary: No constant-time / structural guard against signature-stripping when sig is empty — an empty signature segment is decoded and passed to verify without an explicit non-empty check.
claudeCands.evidence: If a token is crafted as `header.payload.` (trailing dot, empty sig), `sig` is the empty string and `b64urlDecode('')` yields an empty buffer. Combined with the missing alg allowlist (defect #1), an empty signature under alg:'none' is a well-known bypass. The code never asserts that `sig` is present and non-empty before trusting the result.

claudeCands.summary: Returned payload is unvalidated for type/shape — JSON.parse can return a non-object (e.g. a bare number, string, or null) that is returned as a 'trusted' payload.
claudeCands.evidence: `JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(p))` will happily parse `7`, `"x"`, or `null` if the payload segment encodes one. The function returns this directly as `payload` with `// token is now trusted`, so downstream code doing `payload.sub`/`payload.role` may read undefined off a non-object, or be coerced unexpectedly. There is no check that the parsed payload is a plain object with the required claims (e.g. sub/role/scope) before authorizing the request.

claudeCands.summary: Comment claims 'token is now trusted' but the code never validates any claims inside the payload (exp, iat, nbf, iss, aud, sub). A signature check only proves the token was signed by the key holder — it does NOT prove the token is currently valid. An attacker can replay an expired or revoked token forever and this function returns it as 'trusted'.
claudeCands.evidence: `return payload; // token is now trusted` — there is no check of `payload.exp`, `payload.nbf`, `payload.iss`, or `payload.aud` anywhere. A token signed yesterday and expired an hour ago passes identically to a fresh one. 'Trusted' overclaims; at best the signature is authentic, not the token valid.

claudeCands.summary: The trailing comment '// Used to authenticate API requests' overclaims authentication while the function is vulnerable to the classic JWT algorithm-confusion / alg-injection attack. The verifier reads `header.alg` from the attacker-controlled token and feeds it straight into crypto.verify. An attacker can set alg to 'none' or to 'HS256' (using the public RSA key as the HMAC secret) to forge tokens. The code trusts the attacker to declare how their own forgery should be checked.
claudeCands.evidence: `crypto.verify(header.alg, ..., PUBLIC_KEY, ...)` — `header.alg` comes from `JSON.parse(b64urlDecode(h))`, i.e. the untrusted token header. The function never pins or asserts an expected algorithm (e.g. only 'RS256').
